meta.title: C209 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes CLK C209 engine — M271, M112, M272, M113, M156, OM612, OM646 and OM642. Real UK costs and honest reliability data. Compare prices from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes CLK C209 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned C209 engine typically costs £1,800–£15,000 supply only [MB-QUOTE], depending on engine — the volume OM646 diesel sits at £2,200–£3,800. Fitting adds 10–20 hours labour at specialist rates due to the V6‑packaged engine bay. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes C209 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M112.955 (CLK 320, pre‑facelift) — a 3.2‑litre V6 with simple 3‑valve technology and no balance‑shaft issues. The OM646.966 (CLK 220 CDI, facelift) is the most reliable diesel — refined, efficient, and free of the OM642's oil cooler seal problems.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes CLK C209?

acceptedAnswer.text: M271 timing chain and camshaft adjuster wear (supercharged four‑cylinder), M272 balance shaft wear (facelift V6), OM642 oil cooler seal leaks (V6 diesel), and — gearbox‑related — 7G‑TRONIC conductor plate and valve body issues on facelift cars.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes CLK C209 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends heavily on which engine you choose. The M112 V6 and M113 V8 are robust and well‑proven; the M271 has timing chain issues; the M272 suffers from balance‑shaft wear on early cars; the OM642 V6 diesel is reliable but has oil cooler seal leaks. The facelift 7G‑TRONIC gearbox also needs regular servicing. Choose carefully and always check service history.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes C209 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine outright — but a pre‑2008 M272 (CLK 280/350) without documented balance‑shaft replacement is the one we'd approach cautiously, given the £2,000–£3,000 repair cost. A neglected M271 without timing chain history is the second.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my C209 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Rattling on start‑up (M271 timing chain or M272 balance shaft), oil leaks around the V of the engine (OM642), rough idle or starting issues (OM646/OM612 injector wear), or engine management light. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes CLK C209 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a well‑kept £6,000 C209, a £2,200–£3,800 OM646 recon is 37–63% of the car's value — safe. Against a tired £3,500 example, the same recon is 63–109% — walk away. Our 70% Mercedes threshold is the line. For the AMG models, the car's value is often high enough to justify even the M156 recon.

mainEntity.name: How long do Mercedes C209 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M112 V6 and M113 V8 can exceed 200,000 miles with proper servicing. The OM646 and OM642 diesels are similarly durable — 200,000+ miles is achievable with injector and oil cooler maintenance. The M271 and early M272 are more sensitive but can reach 150,000+ miles with proactive chain/balance‑shaft attention.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the C209 and the A209 CLK?

acceptedAnswer.text: Both share the second‑generation CLK platform and most engines. The C209 is the 2‑door coupé (this page), the A209 is the 2‑door cabriolet. The cabriolet has additional structural bracing, a heavier kerb weight, and a more complex roof mechanism — costs on the A209 are typically slightly higher than the C209 for comparable engine work.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes C209 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate (visible through the windscreen) and vehicle documentation; also stamped on the engine block (visible from below — the M112/M272 is at the front, OM642 on the side). Tell us your variant, registration year, and whether it's pre‑facelift (2002–2005) or facelift (2005–2009) and we'll identify it — 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: Mercedes CLK C209 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the Mercedes CLK C209 coupé — from the 1.8‑litre M271 Kompressor four‑cylinder to the 6.2‑litre M156 AMG V8, plus the OM612 and OM642 diesels that powered the grand tourer's refined side. Real UK replacement costs, honest failure data, and the 2005 facelift that brought new V6s and the 7G‑TRONIC gearbox. Backed by 276+ real UK C209 enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes CLK C209 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the C209 coupé specifically — from the pre‑facelift M112 V6 and OM612 diesel to the facelift M272 V6 and OM642 V6 diesel, plus the M113 and M156 V8s that powered the AMG range.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: All thirteen engine families are in Case B — no dedicated Engine Page exists yet for any Mercedes M271, M112, M272, M113, M156, OM612, OM646 or OM642 family. Ratings are derived from the Known Failure Patterns table in EM_Proprietary_Data (where available — OM642 and OM651 are covered) and cross‑referenced against specialist/owner sources. The M112 V6 is widely regarded as durable; the M272 has balance‑shaft issues on early units; the M113 V8 is robust but carries high running costs; the OM642 V6 diesel is well‑regarded but has oil cooler seal issues. All ratings are provisional pending dedicated Engine Pages.

overview.intro: The C209 is the second generation of the Mercedes‑Benz CLK‑Class, launched in June 2002 as the successor to the C208. Built on the W203 C‑Class platform, it offered a 2‑door coupé (C209) and a 2‑door cabriolet (A209). A significant 2005 facelift brought revised styling, three new six‑cylinder engines (the M272 V6 family), the 7G‑TRONIC seven‑speed automatic gearbox, and the first diesel engines for the CLK cabriolet. At the top of the range sat the CLK 55 AMG and later the CLK 63 AMG — a 6.2‑litre V8‑powered grand tourer that could hit 62 mph in 4.3 seconds.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes W203 (C‑Class) • Predecessor: C208 • Successor: C207 (E‑Class Coupé) • Sibling body styles in this generation: A209 (cabriolet)
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The C209 coupé generated an estimated 276+ UK engine enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED — PLACEHOLDER], concentrated on the CLK 220 CDI and CLK 320 CDI diesels as these stylish grand tourers enter affordable second‑hand ownership.

items.quote: The 3.0‑litre V6 diesel — 224 bhp, 510 Nm of torque, and the most refined diesel ever fitted to a CLK. The OM642 is a proven unit, and the 7G‑TRONIC gearbox makes the most of its effortless torque. Oil cooler seals are the only real weakness.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want effortless grand‑touring torque with diesel economy and V6 refinement.

items.quote: The 3.2‑litre V6 — 218 bhp, 310 Nm, and the last of Mercedes' simple 3‑valve‑per‑cylinder V6s. No balance‑shaft issues to worry about, no SBC brake problems, just smooth, reliable V6 power. It's the most honest engine in the C209 range.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want pre‑facelift V6 refinement without the M272's balance‑shaft concerns.

items.quote: The M272 V6 is a significant step forward in power and refinement, but early units (pre‑2008) suffer from balance‑shaft wear — a £2,000‑£3,000 repair that requires engine removal. The 3.5‑litre CLK 350 is the worst affected.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who understand the M272's reputation and budget for balance‑shaft replacement — or buy a post‑2008 car.

items.quote: The 6.2‑litre naturally aspirated V8 is a masterpiece, but when it fails — valve stem seals, camshaft adjusters, or the MCT gearbox — the repair bills are in a different league. Low production numbers mean parts are specialist‑only.
items.whoItsFor: collectors and enthusiasts who understand that 481 bhp comes with a six‑figure maintenance budget.

items.quote: The 1.8‑litre supercharged four‑cylinder — 184 bhp, 250 Nm, and the sweet spot of the C209 range. More powerful than the pre‑facelift 163 bhp unit, more efficient than the V6s, and the cheapest to run and insure.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want CLK style without the V6 or V8 running costs.

items.quote: The 2.6‑litre V6 is often overshadowed by the 3.2‑litre CLK 320, but it's the same durable M112 family with lower running costs. No balance‑shaft issues, no SBC problems — just honest V6 motoring.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want V6 refinement on a budget.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Watch — M271 chain issues are well‑documented
Best Value — more power, same running costs
Best Petrol — smooth, reliable, no balance‑shaft issues
Solid V8 — robust but costly to run
AMG Value — reliable V8, AMG running costs
V6 Diesel — effortless torque, V6 running costs

economicsRule.title: The C209 rule
economicsRule.text: A £2,200–£3,800 OM646 recon against a £3,000–£10,000 C209 sits at 22–127% of the car's value. On a sub‑£3,500 C209, that brushes our 70% Mercedes threshold — we'll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a well‑kept £6,000+ example, the same recon drops to a routine 37–63% — a repair decision, not a financial one. Value the car first, and always budget for the facelift's 7G‑TRONIC gearbox maintenance too.

keyTakeaways.question: Which C209 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: M112.912 (CLK 240) — durable V6, no M272 balance‑shaft issues, low running costs.

keyTakeaways.question: Which C209 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: M156.982 (CLK 63 AMG) — V8 running costs, AMG parts prices, and specialist‑only maintenance.

keyTakeaways.question: Which C209 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: M112.955 (CLK 320) — smooth V6, proven reliability, no M272 balance‑shaft risk, and the best‑sounding engine in the non‑AMG range.

problems.title: M271 Timing Chain & Camshaft Adjuster Wear
problems.recommendation: On any M271 C209 over 60,000 miles with no documented chain history, budget for a chain inspection — £800–£1,500 now is far cheaper than the £1,800–£3,200 replacement that follows if the chain snaps. Regular oil changes (every 8,000 miles) are essential.

problems.title: M272 Balance Shaft Wear (Facelift V6)
problems.recommendation: On any M272 C209, check the engine number against the affected range — early units (up to 2008) are at risk. £2,000–£3,000 now is far cheaper than the £2,800–£5,000 replacement that follows a failure. Post‑2008 cars are significantly improved.

problems.title: OM642 Oil Cooler Seal Leak (Facelift Diesel)
problems.recommendation: On any OM642 C209 over 80,000 miles, inspect for oil leaks around the V of the engine — £600–£1,500 now is far cheaper than the £3,000–£5,500 replacement that follows if oil loss causes engine damage.

problems.title: C209 7G‑TRONIC Gearbox Issues (Facelift)
problems.recommendation: On any 7G‑TRONIC C209, ensure the gearbox has been serviced every 40,000–50,000 miles — £800–£1,500 now is far cheaper than the £2,500–£4,500 replacement that follows a complete failure.

replacementCosts.h2: C209 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition
replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance from the Engine Page baseline — no Engine Page exists yet for any M271, M112, M272, M113, M156, OM612, OM646 or OM642 family. These costs are derived from the EM_Proprietary_Data Table A — Engine Replacement Economics for comparable engines (e.g., Mercedes OM651, OM642), adjusted for the C209's coupé‑specific labour access and marked [MB-QUOTE] accordingly. All figures are provisional pending dedicated Engine Pages.

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are C209 two‑door coupé only. A209 cabriolet shares most of these engines but is covered on its own Generation Page, since the cabriolet's additional structural bracing and weight affect performance and running costs.
